subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : When an object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave lens, image is formed at the centre of curvature of the lens on the other side.
Statement (II) : Concave lens always forms a virtual and erect image.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : When an object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave lens, image is formed at the centre of curvature of the lens on the other side.
Statement (II) : Concave lens always forms a virtual and erect image.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{-2 \mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{-\mathrm{f}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{-1}{2 \mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=-2 \mathrm{f} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow \text { Virtual image of Real object. }\end{aligned}$
In statement II, it is not mentioned that object is real or virtual hence Statement II is false.

solution:
Resolving limit of telescope =1.22λD
Where, D = Diameter of lens
λ = Wavelength of light

subject: Physics
q: A spherical interface lens of radius $R$ separates two media of refractive indices $1$ and $1.4$ respectively as shown in the figure below. A point source is placed at a distance of $4R$ in front of spherical interface. The magnitude of the magnification of point source image is _______.
question: A spherical interface lens of radius $R$ separates two media of refractive indices $1$ and $1.4$ respectively as shown in the figure below. A point source is placed at a distance of $4R$ in front of spherical interface. The magnitude of the magnification of point source image is _______.
solution:
Given:
Refractive index of the first medium, $n_1 = 1$
Refractive index of the second medium, $n_2 = 1.4 = \dfrac{7}{5}$
Object distance, $u = -4R$
Radius of curvature of the spherical interface, $R = +R$ (since the surface is convex towards the rarer medium)

Using the formula for refraction at a single spherical surface:
$\dfrac{n_2}{v} - \dfrac{n_1}{u} = \dfrac{n_2 - n_1}{R}$

Substituting the given values:
$\dfrac{1.4}{v} - \dfrac{1}{-4R} = \dfrac{1.4 - 1}{R}$

$\dfrac{7}{5v} + \dfrac{1}{4R} = \dfrac{0.4}{R}$

$\dfrac{7}{5v} + \dfrac{1}{4R} = \dfrac{2}{5R}$

$\dfrac{7}{5v} = \dfrac{2}{5R} - \dfrac{1}{4R}$

$\dfrac{7}{5v} = \dfrac{8 - 5}{20R} = \dfrac{3}{20R}$

$v = \dfrac{7 \times 20R}{5 \times 3} = \dfrac{28R}{3}$

The transverse magnification $m$ for a spherical refracting surface is given by:
$m = \dfrac{n_1 v}{n_2 u}$

Substituting the values of $n_1$, $n_2$, $u$, and $v$:
$m = \dfrac{1 \times \left(\dfrac{28R}{3}\right)}{1.4 \times (-4R)}$

$m = \dfrac{\dfrac{28R}{3}}{-\dfrac{28R}{5}}$

$m = -\dfrac{5}{3}$

The magnitude of the magnification is:
$|m| = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.66$

Answer: $1.66$

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
question: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
solution:
By using Snell's law, n1sinθ1=n2sinθ2, the refractive index is 

μ=sin45°sin30°=1212=2

Also using the relation n=cv where n is the refractive index, c is the velocity of light in air and v is the velocity in the medium

⇒cair v =2=λ1 λ2

The velocities are given by 

v=νλ2c=νλ1

⇒λ1=2λ2 and ν1=ν2.

The frequency of light does not change when the medium changes.

question: A telescope has an objective lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and an eyepiece of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. =32×180°3.14=86°

subject: Physics
q: A thin biconvex lens is prepared from the glass ($\mu=1.5$) both curved surfaces of which have equal radii of $20$ cm each. Left side surface of the lens is silvered from outside to make it reflecting. To have the position of image and object at the same place, the object should be placed, from the lens at a distance of ________ cm.
question: A thin biconvex lens is prepared from the glass ($\mu=1.5$) both curved surfaces of which have equal radii of $20$ cm each. Left side surface of the lens is silvered from outside to make it reflecting. To have the position of image and object at the same place, the object should be placed, from the lens at a distance of ________ cm.
solution:
When one surface of a lens is silvered, the system behaves like an equivalent mirror. The light refracts through the lens, reflects off the silvered surface, and refracts through the lens again, so the equivalent power is:
$P_{eq} = P_L + P_M + P_L = 2P_L + P_M$

Power of the biconvex lens:
For a thin biconvex lens with $R_1 = +20\text{ cm}$, $R_2 = -20\text{ cm}$ and $\mu = 1.5$, the lens maker's formula gives:
$\dfrac{1}{f_L} = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{f_L} = (1.5 - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{20} - \dfrac{1}{-20}\right) = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{2}{20} = \dfrac{1}{20}\text{ cm}^{-1}$

So, $P_L = \dfrac{1}{20}\text{ cm}^{-1}$.

Power of the silvered surface (acting as a concave mirror):
The back surface has radius of curvature $R = 20\text{ cm}$, so its focal length is:
$f_M = \dfrac{R}{2} = 10\text{ cm}$

$P_M = \dfrac{1}{f_M} = \dfrac{1}{10}\text{ cm}^{-1}$

Equivalent focal length:
$P_{eq} = 2P_L + P_M = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{20} + \dfrac{1}{10} = \dfrac{1}{10} + \dfrac{1}{10} = \dfrac{1}{5}\text{ cm}^{-1}$

$F_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{P_{eq}} = 5\text{ cm}$

Since $P_{eq} > 0$, the system behaves as a converging (concave) equivalent mirror of focal length $5\text{ cm}$.

Object position for object and image to coincide:
For a concave mirror, the image coincides with the object when the object is placed at the centre of curvature, i.e., at a distance $2F_{eq}$ from the mirror:
$u = 2F_{eq} = 2 \times 5 = 10\text{ cm}$

Hence, the correct option is $(1)\ 10$.

When liquid of refractive index μl is put between the lens and the mirror, the pin has to be moved to A', such that OA'=27 cm, to get its inverted real image at A' itself. The value of μl will be
question: A thin convex lens L (refractive index =1.5 ) is placed on a plane mirror M. When a pin is placed at A, such that OA=18 cm, its real inverted image is formed at A itself, as shown in figure. When liquid of refractive index μl is put between the lens and the mirror, the pin has to be moved to A', such that OA'=27 cm, to get its inverted real image at A' itself. The value of μl will be
solution:
1f1=12×218
1f2=μ1-1-18
When μ1 is filled between lens and mirror,
P=218-218μ1-1=2-2μ1+218
⇒Fm=-182-μ1
2=6-3μ1
3μ1=4
μ1=43

subject: Physics
q: A mirror is used to produce an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{4}$. If the distance between object and its image is 40 cm , then the focal length of the mirror is ________
question: A mirror is used to produce an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{4}$. If the distance between object and its image is 40 cm , then the focal length of the mirror is ________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{-\mathrm{u}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}} \\ & \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=4 \mathrm{v} \\ & \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{u}=40 \\ & 5 \mathrm{v}=40 \\ & \mathrm{v}=8 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \mathrm{u}=32 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{32}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{4-1}{32}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \mathrm{f}=\frac{32}{3}=10.7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

question:

(Given c=3×108 m s-1 )
solution:
The refractive index of a medium is given by, n=μrεr.

For medium 2, n2=1×9=3

Now speed of light in a medium will be,

cv=n⇒v=3×1083=1×108 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens has power P. It is cut into two halves along its principal axis. Further one piece (out of the two halves) is cut into two halves perpendicular to the principal axis (as shown in figures). Choose the incorrect option for the reported pieces.
question: A convex lens has power P. It is cut into two halves along its principal axis. Further one piece (out of the two halves) is cut into two halves perpendicular to the principal axis (as shown in figures). Choose the incorrect option for the reported pieces.
solution:
Using Lens maker's formula, power is given by

P=1f=μ-11R1-1R2

 

When convex lens is cut into two parts, P1=1f=μ-12R=P

 

When half convex lens is cut into two parts,P2=1f=μ-11R=P2

Power of lens,  P3=1f=μ-11R=P2

subject: Physics
q:
A light wave travelling linearly in a medium of dielectric constant 4, incidents on the horizontal interface separating medium with air. The angle of incidence for which the total intensity of incident wave will be reflected back into the same medium will be :
(Given : relative permeability of medium μr=1)
question:
A light wave travelling linearly in a medium of dielectric constant 4, incidents on the horizontal interface separating medium with air. The angle of incidence for which the total intensity of incident wave will be reflected back into the same medium will be :
(Given : relative permeability of medium μr=1)
solution:
Relative refractive index is μ=μrεr=4×1=2

Now, for complete reflection, θ>θc, where, θc is critical angle.

Now, critical angle is sinθc=1μ=12

 ⇒θc=sin-112⇒θc=30°

So, the angle of incidence for which the total intensity of incident wave will be reflected back into the same medium is θ>30° i.e., 60°.

The resolving power of this telescope, if a light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2440</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math> is used to see the object will be
question: The aperture of the objective is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The resolving power of this telescope, if a light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2440</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math> is used to see the object will be
solution:
Given,

The diameter of the objective lens, D=24.4 cm

The wavelength of the light, λ=2440 A∘

The resolving power of a telescope is given by,

R.P=D1.22λ ⇒R.P=24.4×10-21.22×2440×10-10=8.2×105

Hence, the resolving power of a telescope is 8.2×105.

subject: Physics
q: A pole is vertically submerged in swimming pool, such that it gives a length of shadow 2.15 m within water when sunlight is incident at an angle of 30° with the surface of water. If swimming pool is filled to a height of 1.5 m, then the height of the pole above the water surface in centimeters is (nw=43) ________.
question: A pole is vertically submerged in swimming pool, such that it gives a length of shadow 2.15 m within water when sunlight is incident at an angle of 30° with the surface of water. If swimming pool is filled to a height of 1.5 m, then the height of the pole above the water surface in centimeters is (nw=43) ________.
solution:
Let angle BAC is θ.

Applying Snell's law at E, we get 1×sin60°=43×sinθ

⇒sinθ=338⇒tanθ=sinθ1-sinθ2=3337

Now, BC=1.5×tanθ=1.28

Then, AB=2.15-BC=0.86 m

Required value, DF=AB tan30°=0.5015 m≈50 cm

subject: Physics
q: An effective power of a combination of 5 identical convex lenses which are kept in contact along the principal axis is $25 \mathrm{D}$. Focal length of each of the convex lens is :
question: An effective power of a combination of 5 identical convex lenses which are kept in contact along the principal axis is $25 \mathrm{D}$. Focal length of each of the convex lens is :
solution:
We know that $P_{\mathrm{eq}}=\Sigma \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}$ $\therefore$ given all lenses are identical $\begin{aligned} & \therefore 5 P=25 D \\ & \therefore P=5 D \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{f}=5 \Rightarrow f=\frac{1}{5} \mathrm{~m}=20 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$
Hence option (1)

question: A point object O is placed in front of two thin symmetrical coaxial convex lenses L1 and L2 with focal length 24 cm and 9 cm respectively. The distance between two lenses is 10 cm and the object is placed 6 cm away from lens L1 as shown in the figure. The distance between the object and the image formed by the system of two lenses is _____________ cm
solution:
For L1:

1v1+16=124

⇒1v1=124-16

=1-424

⇒1v1=-324

⇒v1 = – 8 cm

For L2:

u = –18 cm

⇒1v+118=19

⇒1v=19-118

=2-118

⇒1v=118

⇒v = 18 cm

Distance between object and distance = 16 + 18 = 34 cm.

solution:
We know that angle of deviation depend upon the angle of incidence. If we determine experimentally the angle of deviation corresponding to different angles of incidence then and then plot i (on x-axis) and δ (on y-axis), we get a curve as shown in figure.

Clearly, if angle of incidence is gradually increased from a small value, the angle of deviation first decreases, becomes minimum for a particular angle of incidence and then begins to increase.

The distance from the centre of the globe at which beam of light can converge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>.
question: A parallel beam of light is allowed to fall on a transparent spherical globe of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The distance from the centre of the globe at which beam of light can converge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>.
solution:
For the refraction at the left surface, u=-∞, R=+15 cm.

μ2v-μ1u=μ2-μ1R

⇒1.5v1-1-∞=1.5-1+15

⇒v1=45 cm

For the refraction at the right surface, u=45-30=15 cm, R=-15 cm

μ2v-μ1u=μ2-μ1R

⇒1v2-1.515=1-1.5-15

⇒v2=7.5 cm

Therefore, the distance from the centre where the beam will converge is,

15+7.5=22.5 cm=225 mm

subject: Physics
q:
Region I and II are separated by a spherical surface of radius 25 cm. An object is kept in region I at a distance of 40 cm from the surface. The distance of
the image from the surface is:
question:
Region I and II are separated by a spherical surface of radius 25 cm. An object is kept in region I at a distance of 40 cm from the surface. The distance of
the image from the surface is:
solution:
μ2v-μ1u=μ2-μ1R
1.4v-1.25-40=1.4-1.25-25
1.4v=-0.1525-1.2540
v=-37.58 cm
Hence option (4).

subject: Physics
q: A biconvex lens of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in air. Its focal length when immersed in a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn></math> will be:
question: A biconvex lens of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in air. Its focal length when immersed in a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn></math> will be:
solution:
Given, the refractive index of the material of the lens is μ1=1.5, the refractive index of the medium is μm=1.6 and the focal length of the lens in air is fa=20 cm.

In air:

1fa=μ1-12R

In liquid:

1fm=μ1μm-12R

The focal length as measured in air and that as measured inside the liquid medium are related by, 

fmfa=μ1-1μmμ1-μm   ...1

Equation (1) implies that

fm20=(1.5-1)1.6(1.5-1.6)⇒fm=-160 cm

subject: Physics
q: An upright object is placed at a distance of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convergent lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> A convergent mirror of focal length <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at a distance of <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on the other side of the lens. The position and size of the final image will be:
question: An upright object is placed at a distance of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convergent lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> A convergent mirror of focal length <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at a distance of <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on the other side of the lens. The position and size of the final image will be:
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent lens, twice the size of the object
<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the convergent mirror, twice the size of the object

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent lens, same size of the object

<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent mirror, same size of the object
solution:
First refraction, u=-40 cm; f=+20 cm
From lens formula, 1v-1u=1f
1v-1-40=120⇒1v=120-140
v=+40 cm
m1=-1
First reflection, u=-20 cm;f=-10 cm
From mirror formula, 1v+1u=1f
1v-120=1-10
1v=1-10+120⇒v=-20 cm
m2=-1
Second refraction, u=-40 cm,  f=+20 cm
From lens formula, 1v-1-40=120⇒v=+40 cm
m3=-1
mtotal=m1×m2×m3=-1
Finally, image will be at distance 40 cm  from lens and is of same size as object.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : An electron microscope can achieve better resolving power than an optical microscope.

Reason R : The de Broglie's wavelength of the electrons emitted from an electron gun is much less than wavelength of visible light.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : An electron microscope can achieve better resolving power than an optical microscope.

Reason R : The de Broglie's wavelength of the electrons emitted from an electron gun is much less than wavelength of visible light.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false.
solution: Resolving power ∝1λ. Since the wavelength of electrons is much less than visible light, it's resolving power will be much more.

subject: Physics

question: The refractive index of a converging lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. solution:
1 F=μLμS-11R1-1R2

If μL=μS⇒1 F=0⇒F=∞

subject: Physics
q: At the interface between two materials having refractive indices \(n_1\) and \(n_2\), the critical angle for reflection of an em wave is \(\theta_{1 C}\). The \(n_2\) material is replaced by another material having refractive index \(n_3\) such that the critical angle at the interface between \(n_1\) and \(n_3\) materials is \(\theta_{2 \mathrm{C}}\). If \(\mathrm{n}_3\gt\mathrm{n}_2\gt\mathrm{n}_1 ; \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_3}=\frac{2}{5}\) and \(\sin \theta_{2 \mathrm{C}}-\sin \theta_{1 \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2}\), then \(\theta_{1 \mathrm{C}}\) is
question: At the interface between two materials having refractive indices \(n_1\) and \(n_2\), the critical angle for reflection of an em wave is \(\theta_{1 C}\). The \(n_2\) material is replaced by another material having refractive index \(n_3\) such that the critical angle at the interface between \(n_1\) and \(n_3\) materials is \(\theta_{2 \mathrm{C}}\). If \(\mathrm{n}_3\gt\mathrm{n}_2\gt\mathrm{n}_1 ; \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_3}=\frac{2}{5}\) and \(\sin \theta_{2 \mathrm{C}}-\sin \theta_{1 \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2}\), then \(\theta_{1 \mathrm{C}}\) is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \sin \theta_{1 C}=\frac{n_1}{n_2} \\ & \sin \theta_{2 C}=\frac{n_1}{n_3} \\ & \sin \theta_{2 C}-\sin \theta_{1 C}=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_1 \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_3}-\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{n}_2}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \mathrm{n}_1 \frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_3}-\mathrm{n}_1=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{2} \\ & \mathrm{n}_1\left(\frac{2}{5}-1\right)=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{n}_2}=\frac{-5}{6} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left(-\frac{5}{6}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
There is a small source of light at some depth below the surface of water (refractive index =43 ) in a tank of large cross sectional surface area. Neglecting any reflection from the bottom and absorption by water, percentage of light that emerges out of surface is (nearly):
[Use the fact that surface area of a spherical cap of height h and radius of curvature r is 2πrh ]
question:
There is a small source of light at some depth below the surface of water (refractive index =43 ) in a tank of large cross sectional surface area. Neglecting any reflection from the bottom and absorption by water, percentage of light that emerges out of surface is (nearly):
[Use the fact that surface area of a spherical cap of height h and radius of curvature r is 2πrh ]
solution:
sinβ=34,cos⁡β=74
Solid angle dΩ=2πR21-cos⁡β
Percentage of light =2πR21-cos⁡β4πR2×100
=1-cosβ2×100=4-78×100≈17%

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of a transparent liquid filled in an equilateral hollow prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of minimum deviation for the liquid will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
question: The refractive index of a transparent liquid filled in an equilateral hollow prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of minimum deviation for the liquid will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
solution:
For minimum deviation in a prism,

μ=sinDmin+A2sinA2

The value of minimum deviation is

⇒2=sinDmin+60°2sin30°

⇒12=sinDmin+60°2⇒45°=Dmin+60°2⇒Dmin=30°

subject: Physics
q: An object is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in front of a plane mirror. The virtual and erect image is formed by the mirror. Now the mirror is moved by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the stationary object. The distance by which the position of image would be shifted, will be
question: An object is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in front of a plane mirror. The virtual and erect image is formed by the mirror. Now the mirror is moved by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the stationary object. The distance by which the position of image would be shifted, will be
solution: When an object is placed in front of a plane mirror, object distance is equal to the image distance. In the first case, the image distance will be 12 cm from the mirror. In the next case, as the mirror is moved towards the object, the object distance becomes 8 cm. Hence, image distance will also be 8 cm.

subject: Physics
q: In a compound microscope the focal length of objective lens is $1.2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and focal length of eye piece is $3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. When object is kept at $1.25 \mathrm{~cm}$ in front of objective, final image is formed at infinity. Magnifying power of the compound microscope should be:
question: In a compound microscope the focal length of objective lens is $1.2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and focal length of eye piece is $3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. When object is kept at $1.25 \mathrm{~cm}$ in front of objective, final image is formed at infinity. Magnifying power of the compound microscope should be:
solution:
Given : $f_0=1.2 \mathrm{~cm} ; f_e=3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ $u_0=1.25 \mathrm{~cm} ; M_{\infty}=$ ?
From $\frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1.2}=\frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{(-1.25)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{1.2}-\frac{1}{1.25} \\
&\Rightarrow v_0=30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$
Magnification at infinity,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&M_{\infty}=-\frac{v_0}{u_0} \times \frac{D}{f_e} \\
&=\frac{30}{1.25} \times \frac{25}{3} \quad(\because D=25 \mathrm{~cm} \text { least distance }
\end{aligned}
$$
of distinct vision)
$$
=200
$$
Hence the magnifying power of the
compound microscope is 200

subject: Physics
q: A concave mirror has radius of curvature of 40 cm. It is at the bottom of a glass that has water filled up to 5 cm (see figure). If a small particle is floating on the surface of water, its image as seen, from directly above the glass, is at a distance d from the surface of water. The value of d is close to: (Refractive index of water =1.33)
question: A concave mirror has radius of curvature of 40 cm. It is at the bottom of a glass that has water filled up to 5 cm (see figure). If a small particle is floating on the surface of water, its image as seen, from directly above the glass, is at a distance d from the surface of water. The value of d is close to: (Refractive index of water =1.33)
solution:
Given
u=-5 cm
f=-R2=-402=-20 cm 
Now
1v+1u=1f
1v=1-20=1-5
∴ v=+203 cm
Then
Object distance d=5+203=353 cm (below water surface)
The image after refraction
d'=dμ2μ1
=35311.33
=8.77
d'≃8.8 cm
